title: Nice to See You Again
description: Learn how to greet people, reconnect with acquaintances, and talk about past experiences and personal background.

summary.grammar: Use the present perfect to talk about life experiences and actions connected to the present (e.g., 'I have worked here for five years'). Use the simple past for completed actions at a specific time (e.g., 'I met her last year').

videos.title: Present Perfect vs Past Simple Explained
Present Perfect vs Past Simple Explained

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Hey, long time no see! How have you been?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I've been great! I actually started a new job last month.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: That's awesome. What field are you working in now?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I'm working in marketing. I've been in this field for about three years.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Nice! I haven't seen you since our last meeting.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yeah, it's been a while. Have you done anything exciting lately?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Yes! I just got promoted last week.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Congratulations! That's fantastic news.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: I have met her last year.

quiz.question: What does 'catch up' mean?
options: To meet and talk about recent events

title: Are You Ready for Your Guests?
description: Learn how to plan events, manage guests, give updates, and make suggestions effectively.

summary.grammar: Use the present perfect with 'already' and 'yet' to talk about completed and unfinished tasks (e.g., 'I have already booked the tickets', 'I haven't called the restaurant yet'). Use 'suggest + -ing' to make suggestions (e.g., 'I suggest booking a table in advance').

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Are you ready for the event tomorrow?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Almost. I've already booked the restaurant, but I haven't ordered the cake yet.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Have you sent out the invitations?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yes, I sent them yesterday. Everyone has confirmed.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Great! Who is picking up the guests from the airport?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: John will pick them up. He has already arranged the transportation.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: What do you suggest for the decorations?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I suggest using simple flowers and lights. That sounds like a good idea, right?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Yes, that sounds perfect!

quiz.question: Which sentence uses 'already' correctly?
options: I already haven't booked the tickets.
I have already booked the tickets.
I booked already the tickets yesterday.
I am already booking the tickets.

title: Have You Ever Been There?
description: Learn how to talk about travel destinations, describe vacation experiences, and discuss activities using real-life English.

summary.grammar: Use present perfect with 'ever' and 'never' to talk about life experiences (e.g., 'Have you ever been to Paris?'). Use passive voice to describe places (e.g., 'The pyramids were built centuries ago'). Use gerunds as subjects (e.g., 'Hiking is fun').

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Have you ever been to the Grand Canyon?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: No, I haven’t. But I’ve always wanted to go there.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: I went there last year. It was one of the most amazing places I’ve ever seen.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: That sounds incredible! What did you do there?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: We went hiking and took lots of pictures. Hiking there is really fun.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Nice! Have you ever tried a cruise?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Yes, I have. I went on a cruise to Alaska two years ago.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Wow! I’ve never been on a cruise. I think I should try it someday.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses 'ever' correctly?
options: Have you ever been to Italy?
I have ever seen that movie.

quiz.question: Which sentence is in passive voice?
options: People visit the museum every day.
The museum is visited every day.

title: How is the dish prepared?
description: Learn how to order food, ask about dishes, and give feedback in restaurants.

summary.grammar: Use passive voice to describe food preparation (e.g., 'The fish is grilled'). Use 'so...that' to describe results (e.g., 'The food was so good that we ordered more').

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Excuse me, how is the fish prepared?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It’s grilled and served with vegetables and rice.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: That sounds good. I think I’ll have that.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Would you like anything to drink?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Yes, I’ll have a glass of water, please.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Sure. I’ll bring your order shortly.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Excuse me, this isn’t what I ordered.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I’m very sorry. I’ll replace it right away.

quiz.question: What should you say if the food is wrong?
Which sentence uses 'so...that' correctly?
options: The food was so good that we ordered more

title: Enjoy your flight!
description: Learn how to check in, navigate airports, and understand in-flight announcements.

summary.grammar: Use future progressive for ongoing future actions (e.g., 'I will be traveling tomorrow'). Use possessive pronouns like mine, yours, his. Reported speech: 'He said that...' or 'She asked if...'

videos.title: English at the Airport – Checking In
English at the Airport – Checking In

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Good morning. I’d like to check in for my flight to London.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Of course. May I see your passport, please?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Here you go. I only have this carry-on bag.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Great. Would you prefer a window or an aisle seat?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: A window seat, please.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Alright. Here is your boarding pass. Gate B12.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Thank you. What time does boarding start?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Boarding will begin in about 30 minutes.

quiz.question: Where do you check in for your flight?
What is a 'carry-on bag'?
options: A bag you leave at the airport
A bag you take on the plane

title: Now you can ...! (Review and Practice)
description: Review and practice all the skills learned in previous chapters through real-life situations.

summary.grammar: Review of present perfect, past simple, future forms, suggestions, and reported speech. Focus on real-life communication and fluency.

videos.title: English Conversation Practice – Real Life Situations

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Hey! Long time no see! How have you been?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I’ve been great! I got married last year and I just had a baby.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Wow, congratulations! That’s amazing.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Thanks! What about you?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: I started a new job and moved to another country.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: That sounds exciting! We should catch up over coffee.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Great idea! Let’s meet this weekend.

quiz.question: Which sentence is present perfect?
options: I went to Paris last year.

quiz.question: What do you say when suggesting something?
